unit_title: Initializing a Repository and Making Commits

lessons.title: Initializing Your First Repository
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to initialize a Git repository in an existing project using `git init` and understand what files are created.
lessons.notes: Hands-on: students create a folder, cd into it, run `git init`. Show the `.git` folder structure briefly. Explain this is now a Git repository.

lessons.title: Understanding the Staging Area and Why It Exists
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to explain the purpose of the staging area and describe how it enables logical, selective commits.
lessons.notes: Use a multi-file example: three files edited, want to make two commits. Show how staging area allows this intentionality. Distinguish from `git add` behavior.

lessons.title: Checking Repository Status with `git status`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to interpret the output of `git status` and identify which files are modified, staged, or untracked.
lessons.notes: Show status output with annotations: what does each section mean? Create some files, edit some, stage some. Show how status changes accordingly.

lessons.title: Staging Changes with `git add`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to stage changes using `git add` with various flags and understand how content is added to the staging area.
lessons.notes: Hands-on: stage specific files, entire directory, all changes. Show `-p` for interactive hunk staging. Explain what `git add` actually does (adds content to index).

lessons.title: Viewing Staged vs. Unstaged Changes with `git diff`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to compare working directory changes against the repository and staging area using `git diff` and `git diff --staged`.
lessons.notes: Run `git diff` (working vs. staging), then `git diff --staged` (staging vs. repo). Explain the diff format: @@, +, -, context.

lessons.title: Creating Your First Commit
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to create a commit with `git commit` and write a clear, meaningful commit message.
lessons.notes: Commit staged changes with `-m`. Show good vs. poor commit messages. Emphasize subject line ≤50 chars, imperative mood ('Add' not 'Added').

lessons.title: Git's Object Model: Blobs, Trees, and Commits
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to explain Git's content-addressed object model and describe how blobs, trees, and commits work together to store project history.
lessons.notes: Retrospective: learners have just made their first commits — now show what Git actually created. Use `git cat-file -p` to inspect live objects. ASCII diagrams reinforce the mental model.

lessons.title: The DAG: Directed Acyclic Graph of Commits
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to visualize commit history as a DAG and explain how immutability ensures history cannot be lost.
lessons.notes: Show linear history (A->B->C) and branching (B->C, B->D->E). Use `git log --oneline --graph` on the repo built in this unit. Emphasize that commits are immutable; you can add to history but never rewrite it.

lessons.title: Viewing Commit History with `git log`
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to view and interpret commit history using `git log` and various formatting options.
lessons.notes: Show full commit details, then `--oneline` (concise), `--graph` (visual branches), `--all` (all branches). Filter by author, date, etc.

lessons.title: Undoing Changes: Unstaging and Reset
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to unstage files with `git reset` and explain the difference between unstaging and discarding changes.
lessons.notes: Distinguish destructive (`--hard`) from non-destructive (`git reset` alone). Show recovering unstaged work via reflog. Be careful about command safety.
